narrative:
## State of the Story: OpenAI Pentagon Deal Controversy

### Key Facts Established

On Friday, March 1, 2026, the Trump administration blacklisted AI company Anthropic and designated it a "supply chain risk" after the company refused to allow its technology to be used for mass surveillance or fully autonomous weapons [3][4]. Within hours of this decision, OpenAI announced it had struck a deal with the Pentagon to replace Anthropic's contract [1][5].

The timing coincided with U.S. military strikes on Iran over the weekend, with sources telling CBS News that the U.S. military used Anthropic's Claude AI model for the attack despite the ongoing dispute [1]. The U.S. government claimed the Iran attack was necessary to neutralize "imminent threats" from the country's nuclear and missile programs [3][4].

### Public and Employee Backlash

OpenAI faced immediate backlash following the announcement. ChatGPT app uninstalls surged 295% day-over-day on Saturday [2], while Anthropic's Claude app rose to No. 1 in the App Store [2][5][8]. An open letter titled "We Will Not Be Divided" grew from a couple hundred signatures on Friday to almost 900 by Monday, with nearly 100 signatories from OpenAI and close to 800 from Google [3][4][5].

### OpenAI's Response and Contract Modifications

Facing criticism, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman acknowledged on Monday that the company had "rushed" the deal and announced plans to revise the contract [5][8]. Altman stated: "We shouldn't have rushed to get this out on Friday. The issues are super complex, and demand clear communication. We were genuinely trying to de-escalate things and avoid a much worse outcome, but I think it just looked opportunistic and sloppy" [1][5][8].

The revised contract includes new language explicitly stating that "the AI system shall not be intentionally used for domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and nationals" [5]. Additionally, intelligence agencies such as the National Security Agency would not be able to use OpenAI's system without a "follow-on modification" to the contract [8].

### Broader Industry Context

The controversy has sparked wider tech industry activism, with Google reportedly in talks with the Pentagon over bringing its AI model Gemini onto a classified system [3][4]. Multiple advocacy groups, including No Tech For Apartheid, have called on major cloud providers to reject Pentagon terms that would enable mass surveillance [3][4].

### Key Differences in Approach

Analysts note that OpenAI's approach differs fundamentally from Anthropic's. While Anthropic sought "specific prohibitions in the contract," OpenAI relied on "citing applicable laws" [10]. Legal experts observe that OpenAI's contract "does not give OpenAI an Anthropic-style, free-standing right to prohibit otherwise-lawful government use" but simply states the Pentagon cannot use the technology to break existing laws [10].

### Market and Strategic Implications

The dispute highlights the commoditized nature of AI models, with top-tier offerings having similar performance [7]. Some analysts suggest Anthropic's stance as the "moral and trustworthy AI provider" may have market value that outweighs lost contracts [7], while OpenAI's association with military contracts could prove problematic for consumer and corporate buyers [7].
